answer:
In Allama Iqbal's poem "Maa ka Khwab" ("Mother's Dream", from *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*), **'khwab'** refers to a visionary dream experienced by the mother-narrator. She sees herself in darkness, encountering a procession of emerald-robed boys each carrying a lit lamp, symbolizing spiritual guidance or inner light. Her own son trails at the end, his lamp extinguished—revealed to be caused by her ceaseless tears of separation anxiety. The son explains that her excessive attachment and weeping hinder his progress, quenching the light needed for his journey (likely a metaphor for self-realization or Islamic spiritual awakening, a recurring Iqbalian theme).[^1][^2]

Elsewhere in Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara*, **'khwab'** (or its translation 'dream') often carries metaphorical weight beyond literal sleep visions:

- Life itself as illusory or trance-like: "This thing we call our life is but a haze, / A dream, a trance, a stupor for our days."[^3]
- Aspirational or fragile visions: "Behold creation's garden bloom, / And with one eye, see thousand dreams consumed by gloom."[^4]
- Meditative absorption in nature: "The mountains, plains, and rivers seem / Lost in a meditative dream."[^5]

In Urdu transliterations across the collection, *khwabida* (dream-reclining/asleep) evokes drowsy, unreal states of the earth, hearts, or cities, contrasting with wakeful selfhood (*khudi*).[^6] The corpus does not cover Iqbal's full oeuvre (e.g., Persian works like *Asrar-e-Khudi* are absent), so further instances may exist outside it.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 29 "Maa ka Khwab" (translation), [p-761301](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/29-maa-ka-khwab/translation/#p-761301).
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 29 "Maa ka Khwab" (translation), [p-4feffc](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/29-maa-ka-khwab/translation/#p-4feffc).
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 15 "Bacha aur Shama" (translation), [p-322266](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/15-bacha-aur-shama/translation/#p-322266).
[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 40 "Shama" (translation), [p-620907](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/40-shama/translation/#p-620907).
[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 10 "Ek Shaam" (translation), [p-cc098d](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/10-ek-shaam/translation/#p-cc098d).
[^6]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 29 "Maa ka Khwab" (transliteration), full chapter [here](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/29-maa-ka-khwab/transliteration/). Similar in ch. 24 [p-1705fc](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/24-fikr-e-insaan-par-teri-hasti-se-yeh-roshan-hua/transliteration/#p-1705fc) and Part 3 ch. 2 [p-46e448](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/transliteration/#p-46e448).
